title: Acoustic neuroma: vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII)
vignette: A 34-year-old man is evaluated in the clinic due to persistent buzzing in his right ear for the past 6 months. The patient also has decreased hearing on the right side. He has had no fever, ear pain, runny nose, sore throat, or cough. The patient does not use cotton swabs and has not been exposed to loud noise. He tried using over-the-counter oral pseudoephedrine with no improvement in his symptoms. On physical examination, there is no sinus tenderness, and mucous membranes are moist without erythema or exudate. Otoscopic examination reveals normal-appearing external auditory canals bilaterally with pinkish-grey tympanic membranes. MRI of the brain reveals an intracranial tumor. This patient's lesion has most likely arisen from which of the following structures? (Brainstem cranial nerves are labeled A-G.)

clues.note: Unilateral tinnitus is an early sign of a cerebellopontine-angle lesion irritating cranial nerve VIII. Persistent, one-sided, and chronic points away from infection toward a mass.

Progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss plus tinnitus is the classic vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma) presentation. The combination localizes to CN VIII at the cerebellopontine angle.

A decongestant trial with no improvement helps exclude eustachian-tube/sinus causes of ear fullness. It is a red herring meant to make you consider a conductive/ENT cause; the normal otoscopy and failure to respond argue against it.

MRI confirms an intracranial mass rather than a middle-ear problem. With unilateral SNHL + tinnitus, an intracranial tumor at the CPA = vestibular schwannoma arising from the vestibular portion of CN VIII.

whatMatters: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss + tinnitus + an intracranial (cerebellopontine-angle) tumor = vestibular schwannoma. It arises from the Schwann cells of cranial nerve VIII (vestibulocochlear), labeled D on the brainstem image.
strategy: For 'which structure did this tumor arise from' with a labeled brainstem, first convert the symptoms to a specific cranial nerve, then find that nerve's exit on the diagram. SNHL + tinnitus = CN VIII; on the anterior brainstem it exits at the pontomedullary junction.

eliminations.label: wrong level (midbrain nerves)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the uppermost labels sit at the midbrain (e.g., oculomotor CN III/trochlear CN IV). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: these control eye movements, not hearing; a lesion here would cause diplopia/ptosis, not unilateral SNHL with tinnitus. ✅ Correct if: the patient had an isolated eye-movement palsy.

eliminations.label: pons-level motor/sensory nerve, not VIII
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: mid-pontine labels correspond to nerves such as trigeminal (CN V) or abducens (CN VI). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: V is facial sensation/mastication and VI is lateral gaze — neither produces hearing loss or tinnitus. ✅ Correct if: the deficit were facial numbness, jaw weakness, or an inability to abduct the eye.

eliminations.label: facial nerve (CN VII), adjacent but not the source
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: CN VII (facial) exits at the pontomedullary junction right next to VIII and can be compressed late by a large acoustic neuroma. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the tumor ARISES from VIII (vestibular Schwann cells); VII involvement (facial weakness) is a secondary, later effect, and this patient has none. ✅ Correct if: asked which nerve is compressed late / causes facial palsy as the tumor enlarges.

eliminations.label: lower cranial nerve (IX/X/XI), wrong function
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: labels below the pontomedullary junction mark glossopharyngeal/vagus/accessory (CN IX-XI) emerging from the medulla. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: these mediate swallowing, gag, phonation, and shoulder/neck movement — not hearing; their lesions don't cause isolated SNHL + tinnitus. ✅ Correct if: the deficit were dysphagia, hoarseness, absent gag, or trapezius/SCM weakness.

eliminations.label: medullary nerve (e.g., hypoglossal), wrong function
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the most caudal labels mark medullary roots such as the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: XII moves the tongue; a lesion gives tongue deviation, not auditory symptoms. ✅ Correct if: the finding were tongue weakness/deviation toward the lesion.

eliminations.label: lowest medullary rootlet, not auditory
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the bottom-most rootlets are lower medullary/upper cervical nerves. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: none carry cochlear/vestibular fibers, so they can't explain SNHL + tinnitus. ✅ Correct if: the deficit localized to the lower medulla or cervical roots.

reasoning: Unilateral SNHL + tinnitus = cranial nerve VIII problem
Vestibular schwannoma arises from Schwann cells of CN VIII
CN VIII exits at the pontomedullary junction = label D → answer D
mechanism: A vestibular schwannoma is a benign Schwann-cell tumor of the vestibular division of CN VIII at the cerebellopontine angle. It compresses cochlear fibers (sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus) and vestibular fibers (imbalance); large tumors later compress CN VII (facial weakness) and CN V (facial numbness). Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are pathognomonic for neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2, chromosome 22).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Neuro > cranial nerves and the brainstem cross-section: memorize where each CN exits (midbrain III/IV; pons V/VI/VII/VIII; medulla IX/X/XI/XII). Then the acoustic neuroma blurb (CN VIII, CPA, unilateral SNHL+tinnitus; bilateral = NF2).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: (1) symptoms → cranial nerve (SNHL + tinnitus = VIII), (2) nerve → location on the diagram (pontomedullary junction). Name the nerve before hunting for the label.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on sensorineural vs conductive hearing loss, tinnitus, cerebellopontine angle, schwannoma? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: The pseudoephedrine/normal-otoscopy details bait an ENT (conductive) answer, and CN VII sits right beside VIII. Anchor on 'arises from' — the tumor originates on VIII even though VII is the neighbor.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: This needs brainstem-diagram literacy: identify the pontomedullary junction and the VII–VIII rootlets. Practice the First Aid/UWorld anterior brainstem figure, labeling III/IV (midbrain), V–VIII (pons), IX–XII (medulla).

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.front: Cell of origin of acoustic neuroma
Cranial nerves exiting at the pons
Nerve compressed late by a large acoustic neuroma

keywords.back: Junction of pons, medulla, and cerebellum (CN VII/VIII)

keywords.front: Conductive vs sensorineural — Weber/Rinne in SNHL
keywords.back: Weber lateralizes to NORMAL ear; Rinne air>bone (normal) in affected ear

firstAid.detail: Neuro > CNS tumors / cranial nerves: acoustic neuroma at the cerebellopontine angle (CN VIII, Schwann cells, S-100+); unilateral SNHL+tinnitus; bilateral = NF2.
firstAid.location: Neuro > CNS tumors / cranial nerves: acoustic neuroma at the cerebellopontine angle (CN VIII, Schwann cells, S-100+); unilateral SNHL+tinnitus; bilateral = NF2.

firstAid.detail: Neuro > brainstem: cranial-nerve exit levels (midbrain III/IV, pons V–VIII, medulla IX–XII) and the anterior brainstem diagram.
firstAid.location: Neuro > brainstem: cranial-nerve exit levels (midbrain III/IV, pons V–VIII, medulla IX–XII) and the anterior brainstem diagram.

firstAid.detail: Neuro/ENT > Weber & Rinne tests and conductive vs sensorineural hearing loss — to distinguish a middle-ear cause from a CN VIII lesion.
firstAid.location: Neuro/ENT > Weber & Rinne tests and conductive vs sensorineural hearing loss — to distinguish a middle-ear cause from a CN VIII lesion.

memoryHook: One-sided ringing + fading hearing + a CPA mass = the 8th nerve's Schwann cells (NeurofibromatosiS-2 if it's on both sides).
